text: The surgeon identifies peritoneal implants on the edge of the sigmoid colon and performs a medial to lateral dissection. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is isolated proximal to the left colic artery, with careful dissection inside the vascular adventitia to ensure effective sealing. A protective sponge is visible during the dissection, and the ureter is identified amidst the redundant sigmoid colon, which requires minimal lateral mobilization.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, isolating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) proximal to the left colic artery. The vascular adventitia is carefully dissected to ensure the vessel sealer only engages the artery without additional tissue.

The surgeon performs additional medial to lateral dissection with a protective sponge visible in the field. The redundant sigmoid colon requires minimal lateral mobilization. The ureter is identified, and peritoneal studding is observed lateral to the tumor. Two tattoos mark the tumor's location, with the distal tattoo below the peritoneal reflection. The surgeon initiates the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection without stents, using ICG for ureter visualization, and proceeds with the right-sided TME.

The surgeon identifies peritoneal studding lateral to the tumor and initiates the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection. The distal tattoo is visible below the peritoneal reflection. The surgeon checks for the ureter using ICG for visualization. The dissection proceeds to the right-sided TME in a narrow male pelvis with a fatty mesentery, focusing on the posterior dissection along the back of the mesorectum.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection in a narrow male pelvis with fatty mesentery, closely following the back of the mesorectum. The prostate is lifted to improve exposure, revealing the anterior peritoneal reflection. The surgeon uses suction as a retractor to widen the anterior space, clearing smoke and fluid, aiming to reach five to six centimeters distal to the tumor for a tumor-specific mesorectal excision.

The surgeon navigates a challenging section of the procedure, using robotic arms to lift the prostate and improve exposure. The anterior peritoneal reflection is visible as the surgeon widens the anterior space with suction, which also clears smoke and fluid. The surgeon performs a tumor-specific mesorectal excision, working circumferentially around the rectum, aiming for a margin five to six centimeters distal to the tumor. The distal tattoo in the rectal wall is visible, indicating the planned transection site.

The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the rectum, focusing on the anterior aspect. A rigid proctoscope is used to confirm the adequacy of the distal margin, with the tattoo visible on the rectal wall. The anterior mesorectum is cleared to ensure the stapler only transects the rectal wall. The surgeon then proceeds to clear the proximal mesentery, starting just proximal to the transection of the inferior mesenteric artery and extending to the chosen margin in the proximal sigmoid colon.

The surgeon clears the anterior mesorectum to ensure the stapler only passes through the rectal wall. The proximal mesentery is taken intracorporeally from just above the transection of the inferior mesenteric artery to the proximal sigmoid. A 45mm stapler is used to transect distally, requiring a second load to complete the transection across the rectum. The surgeon checks perfusion and is satisfied with the margins before making a Pfannenstiel incision to extract the specimen.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal anastomosis, beginning with the transection of the mesentery using a 45mm stapler. The distal transection is completed with an additional stapler load to fully cross the rectum. The proximal division follows, with visible demarcation of the tissue. The specimen is extracted through a fan and steel incision to assess the distal margin, followed by the initiation of hand-sewn anastomosis.

The surgeon orients the proximal bowel with a 90-degree counterclockwise turn, aligning the staple lines vertically on both the rectal and proximal colon sides. The camera is repositioned for better visualization. The surgeon begins the second suture for the anastomosis, with one suture directed to the left and the other to the right on the inner row. The mucosa appears healthy. The fourth robotic arm is used to retract the rectum below the peritoneal reflection, enhancing exposure for the anastomosis.

The surgeon orients the proximal bowel with a 90-degree counterclockwise turn, aligning the staple lines vertically on both the rectal and proximal colon sides. The camera is repositioned for better visualization. The surgeon begins the second suture for the anastomosis, with one suture directed to the left and the other to the right on the inner row. The rectal side is lifted using a suture to improve exposure below the peritoneal reflection. The anastomosis is completed with sutures joined in the anterior midline, ensuring a wide and patent connection, confirmed by a leak test using a 19mm proctoscope.

The surgeon takes the initial inter-row suture on the left and joins it to the one on the right in the anterior midline, tying them together. The posterior outer row suture is used to reinforce the anterior suture line, ensuring the anastomosis is widely patent. A leak test is performed using a 19mm proctoscope, confirming hemostasis and the absence of bubbles, indicating a successful anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test, observing the bowel distend smoothly. A scope is inserted and passes through the anastomosis without any complications.
